full_text:
Real-world data from the Oncosys-OVA clinical trial (NCT06117384) - experiences on genomic and functional HRD testing and molecular determinants of progression during PARP inhibitor treatment

Anni Härkönen1, Matilda Salko1, Fernando Perez1, Sanna Pikkusaari1, Anni Virtanen1,3, Heini  
Lassus1,2, Liisa Kauppi1, Ulla-Maija Haltia1,2, Anniina Färkkilä1,2,4

Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Faculty of Medicine, Finland

Department of Oncology and Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki,  
Finland

Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center,  
Helsinki University Hospital, Finland

Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

Background

Identifying high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) patients who derive the greatest benefit from poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitor (PARPi) therapy is crucial. This study aims to establish optimal homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) testing strategies and to characterise the molecular features underlying disease progression in HRD patients receiving first-line PARPi maintenance treatment.

Methods

We analysed a cohort of 323 HGSC patients who underwent various HRD testing modalities at different treatment stages, with clinical outcomes monitored through a comprehensive database. Genomic HRD status was assessed in 251 patients using the ovaHRDscar algorithm with a tumour purity threshold of ≥30%. Two PARPi-treated sub-cohorts were also examined. The first comprised 28 second-line PARPi-treated patients, evaluated using RAD51-based functional HRD (fHRD) testing. The second included 44 patients treated with first-line PARPi who underwent whole-exome sequencing (WES) and RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) for mutational and transcriptomic profiling.

Results

Real-world data from 154 evaluable patients demonstrated significantly longer first-line progression-free survival (PFS) among HRD patients. Genomic HRD testing of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT)-treated samples failed in 36% of cases due to low tumour purity (<30%), with failure rates increasing from 28% in patients who received three NACT cycles to 38% in those who received four. Analysis of 33 pre-NACT biopsy samples confirmed that core-needle biopsies are sufficient and safe for HRD testing. Moreover, fHRD testing was feasible for small biopsy samples and those with low tumour purity. Importantly, fHRD was associated with prolonged PFS in patients treated with second-line PARPi maintenance therapy. Ongoing RNA-seq and WES analyses for 44 HRD patients will reveal molecular underpinnings of progression during first-line PARPi treatment.

Our study provides new insights into HRD testing strategies and establishes molecular determinants of response and resistance to PARPi in real-world clinical samples.
